cataloging_subject.label: Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The U-M/General Corpus edition-level selection consisting of the 1899 title page and Henslow's four manuscript-derived sections on printed pp. 1-73 and 76-145. It is not the complete 1899 volume, which also contains a preface, Skeat's explanatory introduction, material on pp. 74-75, a plant list on pp. 147-278, and an index; nor is it any one medieval manuscript or a single organically unified medieval work.

catalog_summary.title: Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous medieval compilers
catalog_summary.editor: George Henslow, with commentary by Walter W. Skeat
catalog_summary.date: Medieval source material, later fourteenth to fifteenth centuries, with unresolved dating for manuscript A; edition published 1899
catalog_summary.region: England; more specific composition places are uncertain and witness-specific dialect claims are not generalized to the whole selection

titles.value: Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century

assertion.notes: This is the short title displayed for the edition-level selection; it does not claim that all selected source material is securely fourteenth-century.

titles.value: Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century, Together with a List of Plants Recorded in Contemporary Writings, with Their Identifications

scope.description: Full title printed on the encoded 1899 title page

assertion.notes: The long title describes the complete 1899 volume; the plant list named in it is outside the CME00012 cataloging boundary.

agents.name: Anonymous medieval compilers
notes: A cataloging collective for the unnamed hands and compilers responsible for the four selected source groups; it does not assert common authorship.

agents.name: George Henslow
notes: 1835-1925; transcriber, selector, compiler, and editor of the 1899 source volume.

agents.name: Walter William Skeat
notes: 1835-1912; read Henslow's transcripts and supplied the explanatory introduction and notes. The introduction is excluded from CME00012, but his notes remain embedded in the selected sections.

scope.description: The four medieval manuscript-derived sections collectively, without implying a single compiler

assertion.notes: No named medieval author or compiler is established for the cataloged selection as a whole.
Henslow assembled the four manuscript sections and suppressed repeats in later sections.

scope.description: The manuscript-derived text in sections A-D
Notes embedded in the four selected sections; the separately printed explanatory introduction is outside CME00012

date_statements.display: Later fourteenth to fifteenth centuries, with unresolved dating for manuscript A

assertion.notes: This summarizes surviving source layers and historical editorial dating; it is not a single composition date for the heterogeneous selection.

notes: The title's phrase 'of the Fourteenth Century' is an editorial label and is not used as uniform dating evidence.

date_statements.display: Before 1400 according to Henslow and Skeat

assertion.notes: Historical editorial estimate; not verified against the unidentified physical manuscript.

date_statements.display: ?a1500 in the Middle English Dictionary source citation

assertion.notes: Preserved alongside, not reconciled with, the before-1400 estimate.

date_statements.display: Circa 1395 for two section-B recipes printed on pp. 120-122

scope.description: Only the two recipes mapped by the BL component record to Harley MS 2378, ff. 157r-158r

assertion.notes: This date must not be generalized to the whole composite witness or all of section B.

scope.description: First publication of Henslow's edited selection as represented here

places.label: England
notes: Used as a broad cultural-origin statement for the Middle English manuscript material, not as an exact composition place.

assertion.notes: The broad English context is supported; no single locality is assigned to all four source groups.
The manuscript-derived text is predominantly Middle English even though the XML's coarse TEXT/@LANG value is 'eng'.

scope.description: Title page, section headings, introductions within sections, glosses, and notes

assertion.notes: Latin occurs within formulas, headings, quotations, prayers, and manuscript-derived passages; it is not the dominant cataloged language.

form_statements.description: Predominantly prose recipes and instructions, with a substantive 33-line verse prologue in section C.
Sections A, B, and D are cataloged as prose; the four verse lines encoded inside a section-A editorial note are not intrinsic section content.
Section C combines prose recipes with the 33-line verse 'A preface of this booke.'

assertion.notes: This describes plant ingredients and remedies within the selected sections, not the excluded systematic plant list.

subject_statements.label: Confectionery, culinary preparation, and food preservation

summaries.value: An edition-level selection of Middle English medical, technical, culinary, ritual, and prognostic material transcribed by George Henslow from four manuscript miscellanies and published in 1899 with notes by Walter W. Skeat. The selection is predominantly prose but includes a 33-line verse prologue in section C.

Section A prints the English portions of Henslow's private manuscript A; sections B-D give selected passages from British Library Harley MS 2378, Sloane MS 2584, and Sloane MS 521. Henslow generally suppresses recipes in B-D that duplicate section A.

CME00012 includes the 1899 title page and printed pp. 1-73 and 76-145. It excludes the preface, Skeat's explanatory introduction, pp. 74-75, the plant list on pp. 147-278, the index, and other book matter. The encoded title page therefore names content that the cataloged digital selection does not contain.

The heterogeneous four-part selection is cataloged at edition level. Its medieval textual units are not counted individually because XML paragraphs and Henslow's editorial divisions do not provide a reproducible one-to-one item inventory.

extent.description: Four top-level manuscript sections: A, B, and D are prose; C is mixed prose and verse with one substantive 33-line verse group. The raw XML has two LG elements and 37 L elements, but one LG/four L form an editorial Chaucer quotation nested in a section-A note and are excluded from intrinsic-work metrics. The XML also contains 535 P elements, 144 PB elements, 531 MILESTONE elements, and 726 NOTE elements; paragraph and note counts are encoding structure, not a defensible count of individual recipes or works.

content_parts.label: Section A: Henslow manuscript A
content_parts.summary: The English portions of Henslow's private commonplace book, beginning with cloth-dyeing instructions and continuing with medical recipes, charms, and related practical material.
notes: The four verse lines present in this DIV1 are an editorial quotation nested in a note and do not change the section's cataloged prose form.

content_parts.label: Section B: Harley MS 2378
content_parts.summary: Selected Middle English medical, culinary, confectionery, technical, and prognostic material from the composite Harley manuscript, with items repeated from section A generally omitted.

content_parts.label: Section C: Sloane MS 2584
content_parts.summary: Selected Middle English medical and technical recipes from Sloane MS 2584, including the 33-line verse 'A preface of this booke.'

content_parts.label: Section D: Sloane MS 521
content_parts.summary: Selected Middle English remedies, charms, and prognostic material from the multilingual composite Sloane manuscript.

related_works.title: Medicinal and Other Plants Mentioned in Works of the Fourteenth Century, with Their Identifications
notes: Printed on pp. 147-278 of the same 1899 volume and named by its long title, but wholly excluded from CME00012.
James Britten's review of this plant list does not establish responsibility for the cataloged four-section selection.

evidence.citation: CME00012 XML source in the pinned CME submodule.
evidence.locator: TITLEPAGE; BODY/DIV1[@N='A' or @N='B' or @N='C' or @N='D']; PB; MILESTONE; NOTE; LG; L
evidence.summary: The XML establishes the catalog title, title-page wording, four-section organization, textual contents, edition page coverage, source locators, languages, and mixed-form evidence.

evidence.locator: TITLEPAGE and BODY subtree counts by DIV1/@N
evidence.summary: The XML has four top-level sections, 535 P elements including six on the title page, 144 PB elements, 531 MILESTONE elements, 726 NOTE elements, two LG elements, and 37 L elements. Section C contains the substantive 33-line verse group; the other four lines are an editorial quotation nested in a section-A note.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, CME00012.
evidence.summary: The item page identifies the source edition, displays four manuscript sections, and supplies Medicine, Medieval and Materia medica, Vegetable subject access.

evidence.citation: George Henslow, Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century, with an explanatory introduction and notes by Walter W. Skeat (London: Chapman and Hall, 1899), Royal College of Physicians copy.
evidence.summary: The complete scan confirms the bibliographic title, Henslow and Skeat's responsibilities, four manuscript sections, detailed contents and forms, and the material included in and omitted from CME00012.

evidence.citation: Henslow, Medical Works of the Fourteenth Century (1899), preface and Skeat's explanatory introduction.
evidence.locator: preface, pp. iii-ix; explanatory introduction, pp. x-xv; section source statements, pp. 1-145
evidence.summary: Henslow explains the four-source compilation, complete-versus-selected transcription, duplicate suppression, source ranges, responsibilities, broad provenance, and historical dating and dialect claims.

evidence.citation: Printed photographic specimen of Henslow manuscript A, manuscript p. 26, in the 1899 volume.
evidence.summary: The one-page specimen confirms that section A opens with cloth-staining instructions and provides only partial visual evidence for the unidentified witness.

evidence.citation: British Library, Harley MS 2378, catalog record 040-002032704.
evidence.summary: The current record identifies an English composite miscellany of medical, culinary, and alchemical texts and recipes and supports the section-B witness binding and broad cultural context.

evidence.citation: British Library, Harley MS 2378, ff. 155r-168v, component record 041-002041400.
evidence.summary: The component record dates medical and culinary recipes to circa 1395 and maps two recipes on ff. 157r-158r to Henslow pp. 120-122.

evidence.citation: British Library, Sloane MS 2584, catalog record 040-002114954.
evidence.summary: The current record identifies the 117-folio multilingual medical and recipe miscellany and supports the section-C witness binding without reducing the composite's full contents to the selected text.

evidence.citation: British Library, Sloane MS 521, catalog record 040-002112870.
evidence.summary: The current record identifies the multilingual composite medical manuscript and supports the section-D witness binding while documenting a chronology much broader than the selected medieval portion.

evidence.citation: Middle English Dictionary, bibliography/source citation for '?a1500 Henslow Recipes (Henslow)'.
evidence.summary: The MED source citation dates Henslow Recipes ?a1500, a material conflict with Henslow and Skeat's before-1400 estimate for manuscript A.
The linked lineage record supplies stable entity IDs for the source edition, digital encoding, repository artifact, and four manuscript witnesses used by metadata locators and bindings.

open_questions.question: How many distinct medieval recipes, charms, prognostics, and other textual units are present after Henslow's selections, omissions, summaries, and duplicate suppression are modeled consistently?
next_steps: Define an item-boundary policy that distinguishes headings, ingredients, procedures, embedded charms, and editorial notes.
Align each proposed item against complete manuscript images before replacing the current section-level inventory.
Record Henslow's omissions and summaries rather than treating each XML P element as an item.
notes: The current manifest intentionally leaves numbered_items null.

open_questions.question: Can individual selected texts be assigned distinct composition dates, earlier attestations, source traditions, authors, translators, or compilers?
next_steps: Consult the Index of Middle English Prose, Middle English Dictionary source bibliography, recipe and charm indexes, and modern manuscript scholarship.
Create item-level assertions only where exact passage alignment and evidence permit.
Keep witness copying dates distinct from composition dates.

open_questions.question: What is manuscript A's current identity, holding institution, date, provenance, and dialect localization?
next_steps: Trace George Henslow and J. Johnstone provenance records using the manuscript's physical description and frontispiece incipit.
Ask MED bibliography maintainers and likely repositories for the source behind the ?a1500 citation.
Replace the conflicting historical estimates only when the physical witness or a current authoritative description is identified.

open_questions.question: What exact modern folios correspond to every discontinuous passage selected from Harley MS 2378 and Sloane MS 2584?
next_steps: Obtain complete manuscript images or repository foliation concordances.
Align each Henslow legacy page milestone and editorial omission against modern foliation.
Update part locators from broad spans to exact discontinuous lists once verified.

notes: Work metadata describes the edition-level digital selection; witness-level holdings, facsimile availability, and reproduction rights remain in the linked lineage manifest.
The catalog title retains Henslow's historical wording while date assertions explicitly preserve later and conflicting evidence.
The full-title plant list is modeled as a related but excluded work component, not silently folded into CME00012.
No responsibility is assigned to James Britten because his documented role concerns the excluded plant list.
